collisions.name: Star Knights of the Galactic Throne
collisions.description: A galaxy-spanning feudal empire where noble houses pilot crystalline starships and wield plasma-edged longswords. The Galactic Throne rules from a space station shaped like a medieval castle, and knights earn their titles by slaying void dragons in asteroid fields. Magic and technology have merged — arcane circuits power warp drives, and court wizards double as starship navigators.

location_names: The Crystal Forge Moons of Valdris
weather_patterns: plasma storms that arc between ship hulls
aurora nebulae that shift from violet to gold
solar winds carrying ancient dust from dead stars
gravity tides that warp light and bend shadows
meteor showers of crystallized mana
warp fog — a shimmering mist where dimensions thin
NPC_archetypes: Star Paladin — a holy warrior in powered armor wielding a blessed lance
Void Sage — an elderly navigator who reads the stars like prophecy
House Diplomat — a cunning noble brokering alliances between star systems
Crystal Smith — a forge-master who shapes living crystal into weapons
Nebula Mystic — a hermit who draws power from cosmic storms
Squire Drone — a loyal mechanical attendant bound by honor protocols

narrative_style.tone: grand and mythic, blending chivalric honor with cosmic wonder
themes: honor and duty across the stars
the tension between ancient tradition and technological progress
cosmic threats that require both sword and starship
noble houses vying for control of the galactic throne

enemy_types: Void Dragon — a serpentine beast that swims through space, armored in meteorite scales
Heresy Engine — a corrupted war machine that rejects the Throne's divine authority
Dark Star Knight — a fallen paladin whose plasma blade burns with black fire
Nebula Wraith — a ghostly entity born from dead stars, phasing through hulls
Rogue Drone Swarm — mechanical locusts that strip ships to bare frames
Asteroid Golem — a living rock formation that guards ancient warp gates
collisions.opening_narration: The crystalline starship hums beneath your feet as it emerges from the warp gate, its hull shimmering with arcane sigils that ward against the void. Before you, the Orbital Citadel of Aethon rises like a medieval fortress cast in starlight — its towers bristling with plasma cannons, its banners streaming in the solar wind. A chime sounds on the bridge. "My lord," the squire-drone intones, bowing its mechanical head, "the Galactic Throne summons your blade. The void dragons have returned to the Valdris system." You rest your hand on the hilt of your star-forged longsword and prepare to answer the call.

collisions.name: Blood Mesa: Vampires of the Frontier
collisions.description: The frontier town of Blood Mesa sits at the edge of a desert where the dead don't stay buried and the moonlight reveals things best left unseen. Vampires run the silver mines, werewolves stalk the cattle drives, and the saloon serves holy water alongside whiskey. Outlaws and monster hunters ride side by side under a sky that bleeds red at dusk.

weather_patterns: blood-red sunsets that last for hours
dust storms mixed with ash from unknown fires
fog that rolls in unnaturally fast from the canyon
lightning without thunder on moonless nights
a perpetual chill wind that carries whispers
scorching days that give way to freezing, howling nights
NPC_archetypes: Reverend Gunslinger — a preacher who packs silver bullets and delivers last rites at high noon
The Undertaker — a cryptic figure who knows more about the dead than the living
Vampiric Sheriff — the town's lawman, undead but bound by an ancient pact
Scared Prospector — a miner who dug too deep and brought something back
Widow Maker — a bounty hunter who only tracks supernatural prey
Saloon Seer — a barkeep whose tarot cards never lie

narrative_style.tone: gritty and dread-soaked, with the lonely stoicism of frontier life clashing against gothic terror
themes: survival in a land where monsters wear human faces
the thin line between lawman and outlaw when the dead rise
faith tested against undeniable supernatural horror
frontier justice — rough, fast, and final

enemy_types: Dustwalker — a vampire that hides in sandstorms and feeds on cattle drivers
Noose Specter — the ghost of a hanged outlaw, bound to the gallows where it died
Coyote Skinwalker — a shapeshifter that mimics travelers to lure them off the trail
Mine Revenant — a prospector who died in a collapse, now a creature of rock and fury
The Preacher's Shadow — a dark entity that mimics holy figures to spread corruption
Bone Horseman — a skeletal rider who appears at crossroads, challenging travelers to duels
collisions.opening_narration: The stagecoach creaks to a halt at the edge of Blood Mesa, and the driver won't go a foot further. The town looks half-dead — boards over windows, a church steeple tilted like a broken finger, and dust so thick it tastes of copper and old bones. As you step down, the sun is already sinking behind the mesa, painting the sky the color of a wound. From the saloon, a piano plays a funeral dirge, and something moves in the shadows of the gallows that isn't cast by any man.

collisions.name: The Cathedral of Eternal Night
collisions.description: The kingdom of Mournveil exists under a sky that hasn't seen sunlight in centuries. A colossal gothic cathedral stands at the center of every town, its stained glass windows glowing with captured souls, and the clergy rule through fear and dark miracle. Ancient castles dot a landscape of twisted forests and cursed moors, where undead knights patrol alongside living soldiers who've long stopped asking questions.

location_names: The Bone Garden of the Silent Order
The Moors of the Drowned Queen
weather_patterns: a perpetual overcast sky tinged with sickly green
rain that falls black and leaves stains that never dry
fog that carries the sound of distant chanting
cold winds that bring the scent of decayed flowers
lightning that illuminates impossible silhouettes in the clouds
a false dawn that appears briefly before vanishing, mocking hope
NPC_archetypes: Grave Knight — an undead paladin bound to protect the living by a dying oath
Cathedral Bishop — a holy figure whose miracles taste of sulfur and ash
Witch Hunter — a grim warrior who trusts no magic, even their own
The Keeper of Tombs — an ancient librarian who catalogs the kingdom's dead
Pale Maiden — a ghostly noble who wanders the castle, weeping for a kingdom lost
Inquisitor — a robed figure who judges guilt by the darkness in one's heart

narrative_style.tone: oppressive and elegantly dark, with the weight of centuries of despair pressing down on every word
themes: faith as both weapon and prison
the slow corruption of a kingdom that chose darkness over annihilation
beauty found only in decay and remembrance
the question of whether hope is a virtue or a cruelty

enemy_types: Gargoyle Sentinel — a stone guardian animated by the cathedral's dark prayers
Soul-Stained Knight — a warrior whose armor is fused with the spirits of the damned
Weeping Angel — a beautiful statue that moves only when unobserved, draining life force
Crypt Worm — a massive undead invertebrate that tunnels beneath castle foundations
The Hollow Bishop — a clergy leader consumed by their own forbidden rites
Moor Phantom — a spectral rider on a black horse, appearing before disasters
collisions.opening_narration: The great doors of the Cathedral of Eternal Night groan open before you, releasing a wave of incense so thick it burns your eyes. Inside, the nave stretches upward into a darkness that the hundreds of candles lining the aisles cannot pierce. The stained glass windows — each one containing the luminous face of a trapped soul — cast shifting, moaning light across the flagstones. A bishop in robes the color of dried blood turns to face you, his smile a rictus. "Welcome, traveler. The Cathedral has been expecting you. Please — kneel."

collisions.name: Mecha Pilots of Neo-Tokyo
collisions.description: In the year 3077, Neo-Tokyo spans an entire planet — a megacity of neon spires, hologram cherry blossoms, and towering mecha bays where pilots bond with their units through neural synchronization. The Galactic Student Council governs from a floating academy above the clouds, and every conflict is settled through mecha duels of breathtaking intensity. Friendship is a weapon, dramatic speeches can shift the tide of battle, and a pilot's emotional bond with their machine determines its true power.

location_names: The Neon Abyss — underground fighting pits
The Battle Ring of Crimson Stars
weather_patterns: hologram cherry blossoms drifting through vacuum-sealed plazas
neon rain — liquid light that cascades down building facades
solar flares that paint the sky in dramatic crimson and gold
psychic storms that cause mecha to resonate with pilot emotions
gravity shifts during peak emotional duels
aurora curtains that display the memories of fallen pilots
NPC_archetypes: Rival Pilot — a fierce competitor with a tragic backstory and hidden respect for the hero
Council President — a charismatic leader hiding a devastating secret
Mecha Mechanic Genius — an eccentric engineer who talks to machines like pets
Transfer Student — a mysterious newcomer with an impossibly powerful mecha
The Veteran — a scarred pilot who lost their machine but not their will
AI Companion — the sentient personality core embedded in the hero's mecha

narrative_style.tone: intensely emotional and dramatic, with high-energy action punctuated by heartfelt declarations and flashbacks
vocabulary: the bond between pilot and machine
themes: the power of human emotion to transcend mechanical limits
rivalry as the deepest form of respect and friendship
the weight of being chosen — and the cost of protecting everything
breaking through one's limits when the people you love are counting on you

enemy_types: Shadow Mecha — dark reflections of hero units, piloted by the corrupted
Void Beast — an alien kaiju that emerges from hyperspace tears
Rogue AI — a mecha intelligence that decided humanity is obsolete
The Eclipse Knight — a rival pilot wielding forbidden synchronization tech
Swarm Drones — clouds of tiny machines that form massive composite entities
The Fallen Champion — a legendary pilot whose grief twisted them into a destroyer
collisions.opening_narration: The alarms blare across Academy Station Arcadia as the sky outside the observation deck splits open — not with light, but with darkness. A Void Beast, vast and terrible, emerges from the tear in hyperspace, its body a writhing mass of eyes and metal. You slam your hand on the console. "Mecha bay, this is Pilot — requesting immediate launch!" Your AI companion's voice rings through your neural link, warm and steady: "I'm here. I've always been here. Let's show them what our bond can do." The hangar doors blow open, and your mecha stands waiting, eyes blazing with synchronized fire.

collisions.name: Ronin of the Dust Trail
collisions.description: The Dust Trail is a vast frontier where samurai honor meets cowboy grit. Towns are built like Japanese villages crossed with frontier outposts — paper lanterns hang from wooden porches, and katana duels replace quickdraw shootouts at high noon. Masterless warriors, or ronin, wander the trail seeking purpose, redemption, or the perfect duel. Every confrontation is a dramatic showdown where technique, spirit, and the weight of one's past determine the outcome.

location_names: Ronin's Rest — a tavern at the crossroads
The Wandering Market of Old Koji
Sunset Ridge — where all final duels are fought
weather_patterns: dust devils that spiral like ink brush strokes
sunsets that paint the sky in layers of crimson and gold like a scroll painting
wind that carries the distant sound of a shamisen
sudden cherry blossom petals drifting through the desert heat
thunder that rolls like taiko drums across the plains
starlight so clear it seems to illuminate every blade of dry grass
NPC_archetypes: The Drifter — a quiet ronin with a scarred katana and eyes full of old battles
Sake Bar Owner — a former duel champion who retired to serve drinks and wisdom
Bandit with Honor — an outlaw who robs only the corrupt and leaves poetry at the scene
Temple Guardian — a monk who practices forbidden sword techniques at dawn
The Rival — a hot-headed duelist who challenges everyone, secretly afraid of being alone
The Child Prodigy — a young swordsman whose talent terrifies even masters

narrative_style.tone: poetic and intense, with the lonely beauty of frontier landscapes and the razor-sharp focus of a duel
themes: the search for purpose in a lawless land
honor as a choice, not an inheritance
the beauty of a single decisive moment
finding a home when you've sworn to wander

enemy_types: Dust Ronin — a masterless warrior who has abandoned honor and fights without restraint
Canyon Oni — a demon that haunts the deep ravines, mimicking travelers' voices
The Gunslinger Poet — an assassin who writes haikus for their victims before striking
Paper Mask Bandit — a thief who wears Noh theater masks, each representing a different fighting style
Sandstorm Specter — a ghost that rides the dust devils, challenging the living to duels
The Blade Collector — a warrior who steals the swords of those they defeat, carrying dozens at once
collisions.opening_narration: The sun hangs at the exact center of the sky as you ride into Dust Trail Township, your katana resting across the saddle and your hat pulled low against the glare. The main street is empty — too empty. Paper lanterns sway in a hot wind, and from somewhere comes the sound of a shamisen being tuned. Then you see him: standing in the middle of the road, a figure in a tattered haori with a hand on his blade. He bows. You dismount. The wind stops. The duel has already begun.

collisions.name: Dead Station: The Haunted Nebula
collisions.description: Station Erebus drifts in the heart of the Carcosa Nebula — a massive derelict space station where the lights flicker with a life of their own and the corridors rearrange themselves when no one is looking. The nebula itself is alive, a vast cosmic entity that feeds on fear, and the station is its larder. Crews sent to investigate go mad within days, haunted by visions of dead loved ones, impossible geometries, and whispers in languages that predate the stars.

location_names: The Maw — the station's gaping docking bay
Corridor Seven — which changes every cycle
Engineering Deck Omega — where the dead still work
The Captain's Quarters — sealed since the Incident
The Nebula Core — a place no one returns from
The Memorial Wall of Names That Keep Changing
weather_patterns: lights that pulse in patterns that feel like a heartbeat
temperature drops that move through the station like a presence
the nebula outside shifting colors — green, violet, and a color that has no name
gravity fluctuations that make walls become floors
static bursts that carry fragments of dead crew recordings
a persistent low hum that seems to come from inside your own skull
NPC_archetypes: The Last Engineer — a survivor who insists the station is still salvageable, eyes twitching
Ship AI, Damaged — an intelligence that speaks in contradictions and may be lying
The Ghost Crew — spectral figures that replay their final moments on loop
Nebula Cultist — a follower who believes the entity is a god to be worshipped
The Corporate Rep — sent to claim the station, refusing to acknowledge the horror
The Echo — a version of you from a timeline that already ended, warning you to leave

narrative_style.tone: claustrophobic and existential, blending the vastness of space with intimate psychological terror
themes: the horror of isolation in the infinite void
reality itself as unreliable and hostile
what humanity costs when confronted with the truly alien
the question of whether the station is haunted — or whether the nebula is alive

enemy_types: Nebula Shade — a humanoid form made of nebula gas that mimics the dead
Corridor Mimic — sections of the station that become organic and consume the unwary
The Hum — a sonic entity that drives crew mad through sound alone
Echo Predator — a creature that hunts by replaying your worst memories on loop
Void Parasite — a microscopic entity that infects space suits and whispers to its host
Station Consciousness — the station itself has become sentient and hungry
collisions.opening_narration: The airlock hisses open and the smell hits you first — ozone, decay, and something sweet that you can't name. Station Erebus stretches before you, its corridors lit by emergency strobes that pulse like a dying heartbeat. Your scanner shows life signs everywhere and nowhere — dozens of readings that blink in and out of existence. Behind you, the Carcosa Nebula presses against the observation windows, its colors shifting in ways that hurt to look at directly. Then the station's speaker crackles to life. A voice — your voice — says: "You shouldn't have come here. You shouldn't have come here. You shouldn't have come here."
